turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: We will hear argument now in Number 86-1294, William H. Webster v. John Doe. General Fried, you may proceed whenever you are ready.

turns.name: CHARLES FRIED
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: In this case, the Director of the Central Intelligence Agency dismissed from his employment Doe, who had been employed in the Agency for some nine years. At the time he was dismissed, he held a covert position as an electronics technician. In January of the year in which he was dismissed, he voluntarily informed security officials that he was a homosexual; and after considerable inquiry, negotiation and discussion, in May of that year he was informed that the Director of Central Intelligence had decided to terminate his employment pursuant to Section 102(C) of the Act, which speaks of termination with the Director deems that such action is necessary or advisable in the interests of the United States. Doe brought suit, seeking reinstatement or reconsideration of the decision. The Court of Appeals held that there were no procedural defects in the procedures below, but did state that there was unclarity about the basis for the Director's action. It was possible, in the Court of Appeals' view, that Doe had been dismissed pursuant to a general policy of dismissing all homosexual employees. Again, they found further unclarity there. To the extent that such a policy dealt with employees who had engaged in homosexual activities, which Doe had stated he had since the year 1976, the Court of Appeals felt that the matter was foreclosed by Circuit precedent in the case of Dronenburq v. Zech. However, the Court of Appeals thought it possible that Doe had been dismissed on the basis of a general policy for dismissing all employees with a homosexual orientation. And in that event, it became incumbent upon the Agency to explain, in the Court's words, "at the very least," why such a policy was necessary and advisable in the interest of the United States. The Court of Appeals concluded, of course, that, to the extent that these questions must be pursued in the District Court, that review was not precluded under the Administrative Procedure Act by Section 102(C). It has been our position in all courts and it is our position today that Section 102(C) of the National Security Act of 1947, which sets up the Central Intelligence Agency, precludes judicial review, under the APA, of this question. It is important at the outset I think to have a sense of the structure of the 1947 Act as a whole. It is quite a short statute. It is a statute which establishes the Agency. The first provision says there shall be a Central Intelligence Agency and a director and a deputy director, and then goes on to say what will happen if the director is a member of the military. The next section, which is Subsection (C), the one in question here, which is the very first substantive section of the Act, states that, notwithstanding the provisions of Section 751, or the provisions of any other law, the Director of Central Intelligence may, in his discretion, terminate the employment of any officer or employee of the Agency whenever he shall deem such termination necessary or advisable in the interests of the United States. That is the first substantive section of the Act setting up the Agency. The next section, the very next section, sets out the powers and duties of the director and the Agency, and that section contains the provision which was the subject of this Court's recent decision in CIA v. Sims. It is that section which states that the Director of Central Intelligence shall be responsible for protecting intelligent sources and methods from unauthorized disclosure. In our view, these two sections are really flip sides of each other, that the general injunction upon the Director to protect the intelligence sources and methods of the United States implies, and then that it has set out the specific duty to protect the integrity and security of the CIA work force. It seems to us, further, that the decision of this Court in CIA v. Sims, which dealt with the latter provision, sets the tone and sets the context for the consideration of the case here. In the Sims case, the Court of Appeals, the same Court of Appeals as decided this case, while recognizing the very special and delicate position of the Agency, went on to give that provision what this Court described as a "crabbed interpretation," an interpretation which would allow a considerable amount of what I would call rummaging around in the business of the Agency in litigation, through deposition, through discovery, through testimony, document production, and the like.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Fried, I am not sure that I know exactly how to interpret the majority opinion of the Court of Appeals in this case. Do you think that Court opened it up for courts to determine in each instance under 102 whether the decision was in the interest of the United States?

turns.name: CHARLES FRIED
turns.text: I think it certainly did. What the Court said was that there was a possibility that the firing was on the basis of a general policy relating to employees with a homosexual orientation. Therefore, it was open for District Court to inquire what the policy is. And, Justice O'Connor, it is not sufficient, I suppose, once you get into litigation, for the Agency simply to say well, we have no such policy. Because the litigant can say, yes, you do, and let's have a lawsuit about that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is it open to the Director to protect himself from judicial inquiry by saying in effect that he intends to give no reason?

turns.name: CHARLES FRIED
turns.text: The Court of Appeals, in one of the more mysterious aspects of its decision, said that if the Director intended to terminate the employment without giving a reason, that that would be all right. But if, however, he was terminating the employment on the basis of this policy, that would trigger the responsibility of the Director to explain at the very least why his decision and that policy was necessary and advisable. Therefore, it would seem to me that it is open to a persistent litigant in every case to say you, Director, did not intend to give reasons, but it seems to us that nonetheless, you acted under a general policy and now we think that policy is unconstitutional and you must at the very least explain why it is advisable.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: If it is correct that the Director intended to give no reason, do you support that aspect of the Court's opinion?

turns.name: CHARLES FRIED
turns.text: I would be very happy to rest content with that, except I don't know what it gives me. I am afraid that I am buying an empty bag there, because the Court of Appeals, while it acknowledge that possibility, I don't think would have been content with a simple statement that that is what we intend. Indeed, it is a little hard to know how the Director could have more clearly indicated that he was not putting forward reasons, and inviting inquiry into the basis of his action, because what he stated was that he is terminating the employment because he deems it necessary and advisable. Now, that seems to me to be about as good a way as any to say I don't want to give any further reasons. And yet, the Court of appeals said well, let's rummage around a little bit more to see what we come up with. And that is what I think is intolerable.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you think that the Congress intended to preclude litigation of even Constitutional claims with 102?

turns.name: CHARLES FRIED
turns.text: Yes, I do. that has been our position right along and that is certainly our position here today.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Even if were for example a policy not to hire a black or a woman or something of that kind?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Justice O'Connor, if the policy related to hiring or promotion, Congress has subjected the CIA, in 72, to Title VII, so that we would not that. I think what we are talking about is termination of employment. And termination of employment is a very special matter, because if you are an employee of the Central Intelligence Agency, you have access to the most sensitive intelligence information which this country possesses. And the only way to remove that access is to terminate the employment. I think that is why such a special emphasis is given to the issue of termination of employment and why it, in the Congressional scheme, this is a Congressional scheme, it stands on a different footing from the initial employment decision or even promotion decisions. The Central Intelligence Agency is subject to Title VII and in fact welcomes the procedures that are involved because it considers that those procedures in their invocation which involve a considerable amount of internal review, maintains the kind of morale inside the Agency which they are very anxious to have. But there does come a point where the Agency simp]y must be able, without giving reasons and without triggering the kind of inquiry, narrow as the Court said it was, but wide as we think it is, without triggering the kind of inquiry in open court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Why would a Title VII promotion suit involve any less rummaging around? I mean, one agent says I've done a better job on my covert assignments, and I can prove it.

turns.name: CHARLES FRIED
turns.text: There are a number of very important reasons. There is confession and there is avoidance, Justice Scalia. Certainly what is involved in Title VII is a requirement to go to the Agency and to pursue internal remedies there. And the fact that there have been a handful, literally a handful — well, seven; I suppose that's a hand and part of a second hand -- of those cases filed, since 1972, indicates that the internal procedures take care of the matter. Now, you can have the lawsuit. The important thing is that that lawsuit itself might be trumped by 102(C). And it is important to keep that possibility open.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I thought you were rejecting that possibility. I thought you were saying 102(C) does not prevent the Title VII lawsuits for promotion but it does prevent this.

turns.name: CHARLES FRIED
turns.text: Well, there would be the promotion and perhaps there might be damages paid, but if it was determined that this person no longer is somebody with whom the Agency is comfortable as somebody with access, then they could terminate that employee.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No, they have not terminated him. They just have not promoted him. He says he is entitled to a promotion. And you say that a lawsuit will lie.

turns.name: CHARLES FRIED
turns.text: It will lie. There is no question that it will lie.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And I say I do not see why that doesn't open up the CIA to the same kind of probing that you are objecting to here.

turns.name: CHARLES FRIED
turns.text: Well, it does, but that is Congress' decision. We are saying that Congress made quite a different decision as to the termination of employment. And I think that is a rational line to draw.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It seems to me irrational. I would think that you would want to give the individual more protection for dismissal than you would for promotion.

turns.name: CHARLES FRIED
turns.text: The person who is interested in pursuing a promotion claim is a person who is interested in working within the Agency and who the Agency it interested in keeping on in some capacity or another. That person, on both sides of the transaction, is still as it were, part of the family. When you have a termination matter, you have a person who at least one side of the relationship wishes to sever entirely. That is just a balance which was struck by the Congress. It is also worth noting that Title VII lawsuits involve particularly serious and traditionally particularly important Constitutional rights. If Congress decided, which it has, that discrimination on the basis of age, sex, race, are forms of discrimination which are simply intolerable, then that is a conclusion which we must bow to. This is not a Constitutional argument we are making.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: All of that goes, it seems to me, though, not to — you have been objecting to rummaging around. It seems to me the rummaging around is going to be the same in both cases. Now, I can see a distinction between the two, based not on the rummaging, but on the fact that the Courts absolutely have no power to require the Director to hire somebody he does not want in his office. That is a different point from rummaging. I could see drawing the distinction between Title VII non-firing cases and other Title VII case on that basis. That is, the Court has the power to prevent him from promoting someone, or from not promoting someone, but he has absolutely no power to prevent him from telling somebody to be gone. But that is not what you seem to be arguing.

turns.name: CHARLES FRIED
turns.text: If the Courts have the power, as the Circuit Court here asserted, to tell the Director, you may not tell someone to be gone, then what follows is the mischievous rummaging which concerns us. Now, there is no doubt, the Agency is going to be subject to lawsuits in a variety of areas. Congress did not say everything the Agency does, whatever it might be, and to whomever it may do it, is absolutely precluded from review. And my argument should not be taken as asserting that. What I am asserting is that Congress drew a line. Perhaps the line should have been drawn somewhere else, as a matter of policy. But Congress drew the line where it drew it. And we are saying that where it has drawn that line, it should be respected. And the Court of Appeals, while it gave a tip of the that to the important policies there, in effect authorized the most extensive kind of inguiries. And that, we think, is what Congress intended to preclude in 102(C), though, to be sure, they did not intend to preclude it in respect to Title VII actions. So be it. That is Congress' decision. But here we have the Executive and the Congress lined up together, rather firmly, for the proposition --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Fried, I am a little puzzled. They did not intend to preclude it in Title VII actions, but then you say only Title VII actions involving promotions, but not Title VII actions involving discharge?

turns.name: CHARLES FRIED
turns.text: That is correct. That is correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But is that question presented in your certiorari petition? You just asked about review pursuant to the APA, I thought.

turns.name: CHARLES FRIED
turns.text: Yes, that is correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So we don't have a question presented as to whether there can be a Title VII action or a Federal Question action.

turns.name: CHARLES FRIED
turns.text: No. We do not, in this case. I was endeavoring to answer Justice Scalia's question.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What you are saying though is your broad position is that even if the Agency had a policy of just hiring born again Christians and for religious reasons they thought they were the only appropriate agents to hire, that could not be reviewed?

turns.name: CHARLES FRIED
turns.text: No, that is not my position. 102(C) refers to termination.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, they terminated everybody who wasn't a born again Christian.

turns.name: CHARLES FRIED
turns.text: They would have to hire them first.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Sure, they hired them.

turns.name: CHARLES FRIED
turns.text: That would strike me as very peculiar.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Suppose they did it?

turns.name: CHARLES FRIED
turns.text: If they did it, I think they would be in very hot water of all sorts.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But would it be judicially reviewable? That is what I am asking.

turns.name: CHARLES FRIED
turns.text: It is my position that it would not.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It would not.

turns.name: CHARLES FRIED
turns.text: But there is no doubt that this Court would be pressed, and I think might very well be tempted, to reach a different conclusion in that case, because we have here not one, but two questions. We have, first of all, the statutory question: does 102(C) preclude judicial review? And I am quite clear that 102(C) would preclude judicial review even if the claim were made that they first hire and then terminate all employees who are not born again Christians. This Court might nonetheless conclude that 102(C), as applied to that circumstance, is unconstitutional, as applied. They might so conclude. But I do not think that that extreme, but perhaps useful, ability to keep the door unlocked though closed applies in this case with these claims.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Assuming, and I am not suggesting that it is, but assuming a policy of denying employment to homosexuals was unconstitutional, why would the review issue be different than denying employment to anyone who is not a born again Christian?

turns.name: CHARLES FRIED
turns.text: Once again, it is not a question of denying. It is a question of terminating.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Terminating. Sure. You have a change of administration. The prior administration hired all these people. The new administration comes in and says we just don't think we want anybody except born again Christians and no homosexuals. If it is unconstitutional to do that, you seem to concede it would be reviewable on the religious issue but not on the other issue.

turns.name: CHARLES FRIED
turns.text: I do not say —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: We might be pressed to find it was.

turns.name: CHARLES FRIED
turns.text: I am quite clear that 102(C) expresses a Congressional intention to preclude review. I cannot predict what this Court would say if that claim -- religious discrimination -- were brought to it and the question was whether 102(C) was Constitutional as applied to that claim.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I am puzzled by the footnote that your opponents have on Page 16 of their brief about the quotes from the District Court where the Government lawyers seem to say claim is Constitutional. We say that it is. And if we are correct, then to that extent, the matter is insulated from the Constitution if that is how one wishes to put it. But you are never insulated —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: One can be insulated from our review without being insulated from the Constitution, I take it. The President, in deciding whether this policy should be adopted, about homosexual firing in the CIA certainly has to sit down and think, does the Constitution permit this, doesn't he? He could not do it if the Constitution in his view prohibited it, could he? I thought your brief said you are bound by the Constitution, but your judgment in this narrow area of the CIA is final, as opposed to our judgment being final, where it normally is.

turns.name: CHARLES FRIED
turns.text: There is no doubt that the Director of the Central Intelligence Agency takes an oath to support the Constitution and that 102(C) is a review preclusion statute, not something which says that the Director of the Central Intelligence Agency is somehow not subject to the Constitution he takes an oath to uphold.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: General Fried, you say that 102(C) prevents review by the Courts of a claim that the termination was accomplished unconstitutionally.

turns.name: CHARLES FRIED
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That is discriminated, or no hearing, something like that.

turns.name: CHARLES FRIED
turns.text: that is correct. That is correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I have trouble with this "insulate" word. Who else in the United States is insulated from the Constitution, other than the Director of the CIA?f

turns.name: CHARLES FRIED
turns.text: I think many persons are insulated from judicial review of their actions. No one is insulated from the Constitution.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You said he was.

turns.name: CHARLES FRIED
turns.text: If I did, I misspoke, Justice Marshall.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I am quibbling with your language. That is what I am quibbling with.

turns.name: CHARLES FRIED
turns.text: I beg your pardon?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I am quibbling with your language, that he is insulated from the Constitution.

turns.name: CHARLES FRIED
turns.text: I welcome the correction, because I would not want to make that assertion. He is not insulated from the Constitution. He is insulated in a very narrow area from judicial review of his decisions, but he is not insulated from the Constitution.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Who else has that insulation?

turns.name: CHARLES FRIED
turns.text: I suppose the President. If the In 1947, the language was "...in his discretion." In 1964, in the Act setting up the National Security Agency, the language is "...in his discretion, and it is final." In 1984, in the statute setting up the Defense Intelligence Agency, the language was "...final and shall not be appealed outside the Department of Defense." To our minds, the language changes. The thought is the same. The boilerplate gets thicker, but it seems quite clear that in 1798, in 1947, in 1964, in 1984, Congress was seeking to accomplish the same result. If that is not so, then we have the anomaly, an anomaly which Congress clearly stated in various reports, it did not imagine it was facing, that the Secretary of Defense, in respect to the Defense Intelligence Agency, or the National Security Agency, has this unreviewable power, but the Director of the Central Intelligence Agency does not. It seems to us that that is not a sensible scheme, and it is not the scheme which Congress imagined it was enacting. If I may save the remainder of my time for rebuttal.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Thank you, General Fried. We will hear now from you, Mr. Lynch.

turns.name: MARK H. LYNCH
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: The only claim that Mr. Doe has that survives the Court of Appeals Opinion is the decisions of the Court of Appeals that he has an arguable Constitutional claim, if in fact the Agency has a policy of discharging people on the basis of homosexual orientation. So the question then is whether Section 102(C) of the National Security Act of 1947 precludes judicial review of that Constitutional claim. Now, whether or not the Government conceded that there could be review of Constitutional claims, I think they did in the District Court. It is less clear in the Court of Appeals. But whether they conceded it or not, it is absolutely clear that that issue was not litigated, because as the case progressed to the District Court, and in the Court of Appeals, the question was whether the Agency had followed its regulations, not whether it had violated a substantive Constitutional prohibition. So consequently, the question of whether the statute precludes judicial review of a Constitutional claim was not litigated below. At the least, we think the Court ought to remand the case. If it thinks that the statute does preclude review, at the least it ought to remand the case for ventilation of the question of whether Congress can constitutionally preclude review. However, we also submit that the statute does not preclude review of Constitutional claims. The statute on its face says nothing about judicial review. The legislative history says nothing about judicial review. What the statute does say is that notwithstanding the provisions of the Lloyd-LaFollette Act or any other statute, the Director, in his discretion, may terminate Agency employees when he deems it necessary and advisable for the United States to do so. Now given the fact that there was not judicial review of termination decisions in 1947, the most plausible explanation of that language was that Congress was intending to keep CIA employees who had been terminated out of the Civil Service Commission, where they were entitled to go under the Veterans Preference Act which had been passed in 1944. That seems to be a very plausible, rational Congressional expectation. The CIA, after all, in the beginning, in 1947, was going to be heavily populated with Veterans in the Office of Strategic Services. They were Veterans. Without this statute they would be able to go to the Civil Service Commission to contest the Director's termination decisions. And it is quite plausible that Congress would have wanted to prevent that. But, since there was not judicial review of any termination decisions at that point, it seems to me impossible to say that Congress intended to preclude review in 1947.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Was there not some review under the Back Pay Act, over a period of years?

turns.name: MARK H. LYNCH
turns.text: I do not believe so Your Honor. Not that early. I think the Back Pay Act came along a little later. I do not know the precise year but my understanding is that there was no review at all in 1947.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But that theory, that they just used it to keep it out of the Civil Service Commission review, that would not just apply to Constitutional claims. Why does that lead to a conclusion at least that only Constitutional claims can be --

turns.name: MARK H. LYNCH
turns.text: There is also the question whether the statute precludes review of Agency regulations.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Non-Constitutional claims.

turns.name: MARK H. LYNCH
turns.text: Non-Constitutional claims.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What is your position on that?

turns.name: MARK H. LYNCH
turns.text: Our position is that it does not. In fact, the Government's position, stated at Page 18 of their reply brief in the Court of Appeals, is that the statute does not preclude review of actions alleging that the Agency has failed to follow its regulations. They accept the rule of Service v. Dulles and Vitarelli v. Seaton, that they have to follow their own regulations. They also accept the fact that you get review of personnel decisions under Title VII, short of termination. Mr. Fried does not really concede it but he suspects that if there were certain kinds of Constitutional claims, this Court would also grant review. I might also say that another provision of the National Security Act of 1947 which Mr. Fried neglected to discus is the CIA Information Act of 1984, which is an amendment to the National Security Act of 1947. That statute, which was passed after the Sims decision, makes it very clear that the Agency is subject to judicial review under the Freedom of Information Act. So in many respects, the Agency is subject to judicial review. Now, in view of the lack of any textual support for preclusion, any legislative history support, what the Government is really arguing is that the Congress must have insulated this very sensitive Agency from judicial review. But as I have just pointed out, in a number of other significant respects, the Agency clearly is subject to judicial review. So this "rummaging around" argument does not, with respect, hold water.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Lynch, as I understand the Government's argument, it is not so much that 102(C) in so many words insulates from judicial review but when you apply it through the APA, by reading the APA in the context of 102(C), you find that there is no judicial review.

turns.name: MARK H. LYNCH
turns.text: That is why it is so important to focus on the solitary Constitutional issue that is at stake here. Even if Section 102(C) met the standards of either Section 701(A)(1) or (A)(2), for preclusion, even if it did, and I do not think it did, but even if it did, that would not preclude, that would not resolve the question of whether a Constitutional claim is precluded. Because quite apart from the Administrative Procedure Act and the exemptions to the Administrative Procedure Act, people who are directly injured by the unconstitutional action of an Executive Agency officer can get review of that claim in the Courts. You need a statute that very clearly, and very, very clearly sets forth an intent to preclude review of a Constitutional claim.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I thought the question presented here, and maybe it is a broader question, but I thought the question presented was whether judicial review is available under the Administrative Procedure Act, 5 U.S.C. Section 701?

turns.name: MARK H. LYNCH
turns.text: I understand that is the question they have written. But given the posture of the case —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I thought that is the question we accepted certiorari on?

turns.name: MARK H. LYNCH
turns.text: Yes, but you cannot change the posture of the case, Judge. The only issue that is left in this case is a Constitutional claim. Now, whether Section 702 meets the standards of Section 701, you still have that Constitutional claim. I think the way the Government justifies its phrasing of the —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I thought one of the nice things about being on this Court was we only had to answer the questions we decided to answer.

turns.name: MARK H. LYNCH
turns.text: That is true, but you still have to deal with the cases as they come up here. I think the way the Government justifies the way it has phrased the petition is that they seem to argue that the APA can withdraw jurisdiction to review Constitutional claims. That again is another issue that was never litigated below. And it seems to me that proposition is plainly incorrect. Congress cannot in the APA, or it did not in the APA, affect the jurisdiction of the Courts to hear claims by individuals that their Constitutional rights were violated.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Lynch, let me just read you two sentences from Page 32A of the Government's Appendix to the petition,, which is the first two sentences of the conclusion of the Court of Appeals Majority Opinion. Section 102(C) terminations are subject to judicial review. Because the statute leaves the decision of whether an individual's employment should be terminated as necessary or advisable in the interest of the United States largely to the discretion of the Director of the Central Intelligence Agency, judicial review must be deferential. Now, they are not saying there that it is just Constitutional claims, it seems to me. They are saying that terminations under 102(C) are subject to judicial review. They say it in one sentence.

turns.name: MARK H. LYNCH
turns.text: Yes, but you have to go back. If you go back to Page 47A, which is Section C-2 of the opinion, there the Court sets out the only claim that Doe has left. And that is whether a policy to discharge people on the basis of homosexual orientation would violate the Constitution. Then, at the bottom of that section, the Court says at the very least, CIA would have to justify why such a ban on employment of all homosexuals was necessary or advisable in the interest of the United States.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What page?

turns.name: MARK H. LYNCH
turns.text: So what Judge Edwards did, in writing the Opinion, was say that to satisfy -- if there is this policy, based on homosexual orientation, in order for the Director to justify the burden on an arguably protected Constitutional interest, the CIA would have to demonstrate that it was necessary or advisable in the interest of the United States to have that policy. But his invocation of those words from the statute is directly in the context of setting out the Constitutional claim.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Lynch, can I ask you a question. I don't find in your papers any square allegation that there is such a policy or that you ever challenged it. Basically, you seem to be challenging the procedures that were applied in your client's case.

turns.name: MARK H. LYNCH
turns.text: We do have such an allegation in our complaint, Justice Stevens. But what happened was —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Where is it?

turns.name: MARK H. LYNCH
turns.text: In the section called "Legal Claims" in the Complaint, Page 12 and 13.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: MARK H. LYNCH
turns.text: There we say that termination on the basis of homosexuality violates the Constitution. You are going directly to the question of whether there is a policy.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Whether the issue that Judge Edwards says is the only thing you can litigate was ever raised by you in the District Court. I don't think it was.

turns.name: MARK H. LYNCH
turns.text: We don't know.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: In fact, a lot of your allegations, your stipulation of uncontested facts, bring out the fact that your client was never advised that there was any requirement about homosexuality. They did not ask him, they did not have any regulations prohibiting it, all sorts of things that seemed to indicate there was no such policy.

turns.name: MARK H. LYNCH
turns.text: The record is in conflict and that is why Judge Edwards sent it back for further development of the facts. When Doe told the security officer the very first day that he was homosexual —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The Deputy General Counsel said we don't have any such per se rule.

turns.name: MARK H. LYNCH
turns.text: First the security officer said there apparently was a per se rule and then the Deputy General Counsel said no. The question is simply in dispute and that is the this Court has not yet propounded, which is so clear in its intent to preclude review of a Constitutional claim that --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: We have had some cases where that kind of an action is barred by the structure of some other remedies like that.

turns.name: MARK H. LYNCH
turns.text: I do not believe a Constitutional claim by an individual directly injured by the unconstitutional action, allegedly unconstitutional action of an Executive officer.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, at least we have held that the procedures that are provided by a statute preclude the kind of an action you have brought.

turns.name: MARK H. LYNCH
turns.text: I do not think so.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Against the United States, it seems to me we are getting into the question of sovereign immunity. You doubtless have an action against the individual officer.

turns.name: MARK H. LYNCH
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So long as the sovereign immunity of the United States is not invoked. But there are many who think that the sovereign immunity of the United States is invoked when you require the United States to hire somebody. Especially, I would think, in the CIA. But that is not like a suit against an individual officer. And if that is the case, that sovereign immunity is DOCKET NUMBER: 86-1294 CASE TITLE: Webster v. Doe HEARING DATE: 1 12-88 LOCATION: Supreme Court, Washington, D.C. Date: 1-19-88 1220 L Street, N.W.
